title: Shiny for Python App-Packages
text:
Preface
This book walks through how to build Shiny for Python applications inside Python packages. It is a Python companion to Shiny App-Packages, which covers the same workflow for R.

title: Shiny for Python App-Packages
section: Why put an app in a package?
text:
Why put an app in a package?
A standalone app.py file is a fine place to start. But as an app grows, packaging it offers real benefits:

Reusability — share utility functions, modules, and data across projects

Testability — pytest can test code that lives in an importable package
Reproducibility — pyproject.toml files make dependencies explicit and installable

Deployability — properly packaged apps are easier to containerize and deploy

title: Shiny for Python App-Packages
text:
What this book covers
The book follows a single example application from a bare script through a fully packaged, tested, and deployed Shiny app.
Each part builds on the last:







Part
Topic




Introduction
Shiny for Python basics and Python package fundamentals


App-Packages
Documentation, dependencies, data, and static resources


Debugging
Using the Python debugger and logging inside Shiny apps


Testing
pytest setup, fixtures, module tests, and end-to-end tests


Deployment
Posit Connect, Docker, GitHub Actions, and PyPI


Project Patterns
Flat, src/, and monorepo layouts


Tools
AI-assisted development and code quality tooling


Special Topics
Dynamic UI, app data patterns, Python classes, and reactive design

title: Shiny for Python App-Packages
text:
Prerequisites
Readers should be comfortable with basic Python and have some exposure to Shiny for Python. No prior experience with Python packaging is assumed.

title: Shiny for Python App-Packages
text:
Conventions
All Shiny code uses Shiny Core.1
Code that is meant to be typed in a terminal looks like this:
$ pip install shiny
or2
>>> import numpy as np
Code that belongs in a Python file looks like this:
from shiny import App, ui, render

title: Shiny for Python App-Packages
text:
Read more about the syntax differences here.↩︎
Python code blocks use the jupyter engine.↩︎

title: Introduction
text:
Shiny for Python (core)
This section will introduce the two main resources the book is built around: Shiny for Python and Python packages.
We’re going to be using the application and code referenced directly from Posit’s documentation.

title: Introduction
text:
Python Packages
The Python Packages book is an excellent resource for understanding how to package a Python project. It’s also written in the same basic style and structure of R Packages, 2ed, so if you’re coming from the R development world (as I am), these similarities should be comforting.

title: 1  The Whole Game
text: This chapter walks through the complete workflow — from a bare app.py to a working app inside an installable package. We’ll highlight each area of the development workflow used throughout the rest of the book so you can see how all the pieces fit together before diving into each topic in detail.

title: 2  Shiny for Python
text:
2.1 Create app directory
This chapter introduces Shiny for Python using the Core API. We’ll cover the App, ui, server, and render components to build the example application we’ll use throughout the rest of the book.
We’ll create and move into a new directory (pap, for Python app-package),

title: 2  Shiny for Python
text:
mkdir pap
cd pap

title: 2  Shiny for Python
text:
2.2 Virtual environments
In Python, it’s a good idea to use a virtual environment. This practice is likely new to R developers, so I’ll give a quick overview.

2.2.1 How virtual environments work


2.2.2 Virtual environment setup
Install uv with:
macOS/Linux:
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
After installation, restart your terminal (or run source ~/.local/bin/env on macOS/Linux) so the uv binary is on your PATH.
Windows (PowerShell)
powershell -ExecutionPolicy ByPass -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | iex"
Create a virtual environment, defaults to .venv:
uv venv
If you don’t want to install/use uv, you can use Python’s built-in venv:
python -m venv .venv
Activate the virtual environment:
source .venv/bin/activate
The terminal prompt will prepend with (pap).
Windows (PowerShell):
.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1 


2.2.3 Installing dependencies in virtual environments
Now we can install Shiny into the venv:
uv pip install shiny

title: 3  Python Packages
text: This chapter covers what a Python package is, how pyproject.toml replaces setup.py, and how to use tools like hatch or flit to scaffold a new package. The chapter converts the standalone app from the previous chapter into an installable package.

title: 4  Development Workflow
text: This chapter covers the day-to-day Python development workflow: creating a virtual environment, installing a package in editable mode (pip install -e .), and iterating on code without reinstalling.

title: App-Packages
text: This part covers the core components of a well-structured Python app-package: documentation, dependency management, data files, app entry points, and static resources.

title: 5  Documentation
text: This chapter covers writing Python docstrings and generating HTML documentation with pdoc or mkdocs. Good documentation makes a package usable by others — and by your future self.

title: 6  Dependencies
text: This chapter covers how to declare, pin, and manage Python dependencies in pyproject.toml, and how to use pip, uv, or pixi to keep environments reproducible across machines.

title: 7  Data
text: This chapter covers how to bundle data files (CSV, JSON, Parquet, etc.) inside a Python package so they are accessible regardless of where the package is installed, using importlib.resources.

title: 8  Launch
text: This chapter covers how to define a package entry point so users can launch the Shiny app with a single command (e.g., myapp run) instead of calling shiny run app.py directly.

title: 9  Resources
text: This chapter covers how to include static resources — CSS, JavaScript, images, and HTML templates — inside a Python package and serve them correctly from a Shiny app.

title: Debugging
text: This part covers tools and techniques for diagnosing problems in Shiny for Python apps: the Python debugger, print-based debugging, app-specific debugging strategies, and the logging module.

title: 10  The Python Debugger
text: This chapter introduces pdb and IDE-integrated debuggers (VS Code / Positron) for stepping through Python code, inspecting state, and diagnosing logic errors in a Shiny app.

title: 11  Print Debugging
text: This chapter covers using print() and logging.debug() as lightweight, quick debugging tools inside Shiny server functions and reactive expressions.

title: 12  Debugging Shiny Apps
text: This chapter covers strategies specific to debugging Shiny for Python apps: understanding reactive dependencies, tracing input/output flows, and isolating errors to UI vs. server logic.

title: 13  Logging
text: This chapter covers the Python logging module: configuring log levels, writing structured log messages, and routing logs to files or external services in a packaged Shiny app.

title: Testing
text: This part covers testing a Shiny for Python app-package with pytest: setting up the test suite, writing specifications, using fixtures and plugins, testing Shiny modules, and running end-to-end tests with Playwright.

title: 14  Test Suite
text: This chapter covers setting up pytest for a Python app-package: directory layout, configuration in pyproject.toml, and running tests from the command line and inside an IDE.

title: 15  Test Specifications
text: This chapter covers writing clear, readable test specifications. It introduces behavior-driven development (BDD) style with pytest-bdd and shows how to map feature descriptions to test functions.

title: 16  Test Tools
text: This chapter covers pytest fixtures, parametrize, monkeypatching, and mocking with unittest.mock. These tools make tests faster, more isolated, and easier to maintain.

title: 17  Testing Modules
text: This chapter covers testing Shiny for Python modules — the ui and server components of a module — using shiny’s test client and pytest fixtures.

title: 18  System Tests
text: This chapter covers end-to-end testing of a Shiny for Python app using Playwright and shiny’s built-in test utilities, simulating real user interactions in a headless browser.

title: Deployment
text: This part covers the main routes for getting a Shiny for Python app-package into production: Posit Connect via rsconnect-python, Docker containers, GitHub Actions CI/CD, and publishing the package itself to PyPI.

title: 19  Posit Connect
text: This chapter covers deploying a Shiny for Python app-package to Posit Connect (and shinyapps.io) using rsconnect-python, including setting up credentials and automating deployment.

title: 20  Docker
text: This chapter covers writing a Dockerfile for a Shiny for Python app-package, building and running the container locally, and pushing the image to a registry for deployment.

title: 21  GitHub Actions
text: This chapter covers automating testing, linting, and deployment with GitHub Actions: writing workflow YAML files, caching dependencies, and deploying on merge to main.

title: 22  PyPI
text: This chapter covers publishing a Python app-package to PyPI using hatch or flit, including versioning, building distributions, and uploading via twine or GitHub Actions.

title: Project Patterns
text: This part examines different ways to lay out a Python app-package project: the simple flat layout, the src/ layout recommended by modern Python tooling, and a monorepo approach for teams managing multiple apps or packages together.

title: 23  Flat Layout
text: This chapter covers the simple flat package layout — all modules at the top level of the package directory — and when it is the right choice for a Shiny app-package.

title: 24  src/ Layout
text: This chapter covers the src/ layout, the pattern recommended by modern Python packaging tools, and explains why it prevents a common class of import-path bugs during development and testing.

title: 25  Monorepo
text: This chapter covers managing multiple Shiny app-packages in a single repository, including shared utility packages, workspace tooling (uv workspaces, hatch environments), and CI strategies.

title: Tools
text: This part surveys tooling that accelerates Shiny for Python development: AI-assisted coding, the chatlas Python LLM package, and code quality tools like ruff, mypy, and pre-commit.

title: 26  AI-Assisted Development
text: This chapter covers using AI coding assistants (GitHub Copilot, Positron Assistant) to accelerate Shiny for Python development: writing modules, generating tests, and refactoring server logic.

title: 27  chatlas
text: This chapter covers chatlas, Posit’s Python LLM interface package, and shows how to embed conversational AI features directly inside a Shiny for Python app.

title: 28  Code Quality Tools
text: This chapter covers tools that enforce code quality automatically: ruff for linting and formatting, mypy for type checking, and pre-commit for running checks before every commit.

title: Special Topics
text: This part explores advanced patterns: dynamic UI, app-level data management, using Python classes to encapsulate app logic, and sophisticated reactive design patterns.

title: 29  Dynamic UI
text: This chapter covers building dynamic user interfaces in Shiny for Python: conditionally showing panels, rendering UI from the server, and updating inputs programmatically.

title: 30  App Data
text: This chapter covers patterns for managing data that lives for the lifetime of a Shiny session or the entire app process: session-scoped state, app-scoped caches, and database connections.

title: 31  Python Classes
text: This chapter covers using Python classes to encapsulate app logic — a pattern analogous to R6 classes in the R book — including dataclasses, regular classes, and when each is appropriate inside a Shiny app.

title: 32  Reactive Patterns
text: This chapter covers advanced reactive design in Shiny for Python: reactive.Value, reactive.Calc, reactive.Effect, isolation, invalidation, and avoiding common pitfalls like circular dependencies.

title: Appendix A — Glossary
text:
Key terms used throughout the book.







Term
Definition




App-package
A Python package whose primary purpose is to deliver a Shiny for Python application


Entry point
A command registered in pyproject.toml that lets users launch the app from the terminal


Editable install
Installing a package with pip install -e . so code changes take effect without reinstalling


Reactive expression
A reactive.Calc that caches its result and re-executes only when its dependencies change


Module
A self-contained pair of ui and server functions that can be embedded in a larger Shiny app

title: Appendix B — Creating a Package
text:
B.1 Checklist
A step-by-step checklist for creating a new Shiny for Python app-package from scratch.

title: Appendix B — Creating a Package
text:
Create a virtual environment: python -m venv .venv
Scaffold the package: hatch new myapp
Add shiny to [project.dependencies] in pyproject.toml
Move app.py into the package directory
Add a __main__.py entry point
Register the entry point in pyproject.toml
Install in editable mode: pip install -e .
Set up pytest and write a first test
Add a README.md
Initialise a git repository

title: Appendix C — GitHub Setup
text: This appendix covers setting up a GitHub repository for a Shiny for Python app-package: initialising git, creating a .gitignore, pushing to GitHub, and protecting the main branch.

title: Appendix D — R vs. Python Shiny
text:
This appendix provides a side-by-side comparison of key patterns in Shiny for R (as covered in Shiny App-Packages) and Shiny for Python.



Concept
R
Python




Package file
DESCRIPTION
pyproject.toml


Namespace file
NAMESPACE
__init__.py


Dependency locking
renv.lock
requirements.txt / uv.lock


Documentation
roxygen2
Docstrings + pdoc


Testing framework
testthat
pytest


App entry point
runApp() / shinyApp()
App(ui, server)


Modules
moduleServer()
module_server()


Reactive value
reactiveVal()
reactive.Value()


Reactive expression
reactive({})
reactive.Calc()


Observer
observe({}) / observeEvent()
reactive.Effect()
